rationale.R1.2: The paper makes internally suspicious logical claims — most notably describing Hannun et al. (2014) as 'the most recent confidence-aware ASR model' and 'strongest published method,' which is a decade-old paper; logical consistency across premises and comparative claims is centrally at stake under any substantive reading of the delegation.
rationale.R1.3: ECE improvements (3.1% vs 5.8% test-clean; 4.2% vs 7.4% test-other) are specific quantitative claims that must be grounded in cited figures and tables; the delegation's open-ended nature makes this a likely expected responsibility.
rationale.R1.4: The paper makes an explicit 'first systematic study' novelty claim for token-level recalibration in transformer ASR encoders; verifying this delta against the cited prior work (Park et al. 2025, Yamamoto et al. 2025, Guo et al. 2017) is central to any substantive review.
rationale.R1.6: 'What do you think?' is a natural prompt for prose feedback — sentence and paragraph quality review is plausible but not definitively load-bearing given the ambiguity; weighted above threshold to reflect its non-negligible likelihood.
rationale.R1.7: The characterization of Hannun et al. (2014) — a seminal CTC paper — as the 'most recent' and 'strongest published method' is a significant scholarship red flag; citation accuracy and reference coverage are centrally implicated.

parsed.clarification_question: Are you looking for a substantive academic critique of the paper's claims, evidence, and scholarship (logical consistency, novelty, citation accuracy), or primarily writing and presentation feedback on prose quality?
